subtopic_detail: Bronchiolitis severity and respiratory support escalation
stem: You are the paediatric resident on the inpatient ward reviewing a 7-week-old term infant on day 4 of a coryzal illness. He is breastfeeding only 40% of usual volumes and has had two episodes of brief apnoea overnight without colour change. He has no risk factors for chronic lung disease and is fully immunised for age. The night nursing team have escalated because of increasing work of breathing despite low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 L/min.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 °C, HR 168, RR 64, SpO2 92% on 1 L nasal prongs, BP 78/48 mmHg, capillary refill 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: Moderate subcostal and intercostal recession, tracheal tug, widespread fine inspiratory crackles, mild expiratory wheeze, alert but tiring, anterior fontanelle soft
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal PCR positive for RSV, capillary blood gas pH 7.31, pCO2 56 mmHg, HCO3 24 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L, no chest radiograph performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in respiratory support?

options.text: Commence low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 L/min titrated to SpO2 at least 90%, start nasogastric feeds, and reassess after 2 hours before escalation
options.rationale: The RCH Melbourne Bronchiolitis CPG specifies standard low-flow nasal prong oxygen as the first-line respiratory support, titrated to maintain SpO2 at least 90%, with HFNC reserved for persistent SpO2 below 90% or no respiratory improvement after a minimum 2-hour low-flow trial. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence inhaled salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes plus nebulised hypertonic saline 3% three times daily to reduce mucus plugging
options.rationale: The RCH CPG advises against routine salbutamol and nebulised hypertonic saline in bronchiolitis as neither alters disease course. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Switch directly to humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min with FiO2 titrated to SpO2 above 92% without a low-flow trial first
options.rationale: The RCH CPG reserves HFNC for failure of low-flow oxygen defined as persistent SpO2 below 90% or no respiratory improvement after at least 2 hours; bypassing the low-flow trial is not RCH-aligned. The 92% target is a NICE figure, not the RCH 90% target. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Arrange immediate rapid sequence intubation and ventilation in PICU given the apnoeic episodes and the rising capillary blood gas carbon dioxide
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for failure of escalating non-invasive support; the RCH ladder is low-flow first, then HFNC if low-flow fails, then PICU input for further escalation. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min and liaise with paediatric retrieval and intensive care services immediately on arrival
options.rationale: Would be correct only after a documented failure of low-flow oxygen (persistent SpO2 below 90% or no improvement after at least 2 hours); HFNC is not the RCH first-line respiratory support. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: The RCH Melbourne Bronchiolitis CPG places standard low-flow nasal prong oxygen as the first-line respiratory support, titrated to maintain SpO2 at least 90%. HFNC is reserved for failure of low-flow oxygen, defined as persistent SpO2 below 90% or no respiratory improvement after a minimum 2-hour low-flow trial. Salbutamol, nebulised hypertonic saline, and corticosteroids are not routinely recommended.
key_points: RCH SpO2 target in bronchiolitis: at least 90%
First-line respiratory support: low-flow nasal prong oxygen
HFNC reserved for low-flow failure (SpO2 persistently below 90% or no improvement after at least 2 hours)
Salbutamol, hypertonic saline, corticosteroids not routinely used
explanation.pearls: The 92% SpO2 target is a NICE figure; the RCH CPG explicitly uses 90% and notes that brief desaturations below 90% alone are not an indication to start supplemental oxygen.

subtopic_detail: Moderate-severe croup nebulised adrenaline and dexamethasone
stem: You are the emergency department registrar reviewing a 16-month-old non-Aboriginal boy brought in at 02:00 with a 24-hour history of barking cough, hoarse voice and noisy breathing. He has stridor at rest and moderate sternal recession but remains pink in air. His mother says he has had similar but milder episodes twice before that settled at home. He is fully immunised and has no chronic conditions.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 °C, HR 158, RR 42, SpO2 96% room air, BP 92/58 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Audible inspiratory stridor at rest, moderate sternal and intercostal recession, hoarse cry, alert and consolable on mother's lap, no drooling, no tripod posture
data_table.Investigations: No bloods or imaging performed; bedside Westley croup score 6

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg as a single dose, give paracetamol for fever and discharge home with safety-net advice if symptoms have settled within four hours of observation
options.rationale: Stridor at rest with moderate recession is at least moderate croup; corticosteroid alone without nebulised adrenaline misses the rapid airway benefit and discharge at four hours is too early after adrenaline-eligible disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis))

options.text: Commence nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg back-to-back, add ipratropium bromide and reassess in 30 minutes for response to bronchodilator therapy and need for paediatric admission
options.rationale: Bronchodilators target lower airway smooth muscle and have no role in supraglottic-subglottic oedema of croup. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL with oxygen and concurrent oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, then observe in the department for at least 4 hours with attention to recurrence of stridor at rest
options.rationale: Moderate-severe croup with stridor at rest mandates nebulised adrenaline plus a single dexamethasone dose; the benefit of adrenaline wanes by 2 hours so a 4-hour observation window screens for rebound. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis))

options.text: Take lateral neck radiograph and arrange urgent ENT review for direct laryngoscopy in theatre because of recurrent stridor and possible foreign body or epiglottitis given his age and presentation
options.rationale: Imaging delays definitive treatment and provokes distress; classic prodrome, barking cough and absence of drooling or toxic appearance argue against epiglottitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis))

options.text: Provide humidified oxygen via face mask, position on parent's lap and reassess every 15 minutes for spontaneous improvement before considering any pharmacological intervention or admission to the ward
options.rationale: Humidified air alone is no longer recommended and delays effective therapy that meaningfully reduces stridor and admission. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis))

explanation.summary: Croup severity is graded by stridor and recession; stridor at rest with moderate recession defines moderate-severe disease and triggers nebulised adrenaline plus a single dose of dexamethasone. Adrenaline acts within 30 minutes and effects wane by 2 hours, so observation for at least 4 hours after the dose detects rebound stridor. Dexamethasone benefits all severities of croup and reduces re-presentation. Humidified air, antibiotics and bronchodilators are not recommended in viral croup. Children who remain stridor-free at rest after 4 hours can be discharged with safety-net advice.
key_points: Stridor at rest defines moderate-severe croup
Nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL acts within 30 minutes and wanes by 2 hours
Single oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg covers all severities
Observe for 4 hours after adrenaline to detect rebound
Avoid lateral neck X-ray in suspected epiglottitis-resembling presentations
explanation.pearls: Recurrent or atypical croup under 6 months or over 6 years warrants ENT review for subglottic stenosis or anatomical narrowing; dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose is non-inferior to 0.6 mg/kg in mild croup at the lower dose.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a previously well 5-month-old girl who has been admitted with her first febrile urinary tract infection. She responded to intravenous cefotaxime within 48 hours and is now afebrile and feeding well. Urine culture grew Escherichia coli over 100 x10^6 CFU/L sensitive to trimethoprim and cephalexin. Antenatal scans were normal and her parents have no family history of vesicoureteric reflux or renal disease.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 132, RR 32, SpO2 99% room air, BP 88/52 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused, soft non-tender abdomen, no palpable bladder or flank mass, normal genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Mid-stream clean catch urine: leucocytes 250/microL, nitrites positive, culture E. coli over 100 x10^6 CFU/L cefotaxime sensitive; FBC, UEC and CRP within paediatric reference ranges after treatment

lead_in: What is the most appropriate imaging plan from here?

options.text: Arrange a renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks of discharge and no further imaging unless the ultrasound is abnormal or she has a recurrent febrile urinary tract infection in the next 12 months
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months require ultrasound during the acute illness or shortly afterwards rather than at 6 weeks. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Arrange a renal tract ultrasound during this admission and add a micturating cystourethrogram only if the ultrasound shows hydronephrosis, scarring or recurrent febrile urinary tract infection occurs subsequently
options.rationale: Australian paediatric guidance recommends ultrasound during the index admission for infants under 6 months with febrile UTI; MCUG is reserved for abnormal ultrasound, atypical UTI or recurrence. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Arrange a DMSA renogram within 4 weeks of discharge and a micturating cystourethrogram for all infants under 12 months after a first febrile urinary tract infection regardless of severity
options.rationale: Routine MCUG and DMSA after a single uncomplicated first febrile UTI in a treatment-responsive infant is not recommended in Australia. (Source: KHA-CARI Adolescent Paediatric UTI)

options.text: Arrange computed tomography of the urinary tract during this admission to exclude calculus and complex anatomical anomaly given the high colony count growth and the young age of the infant
options.rationale: CT is not first-line imaging in paediatric UTI because of radiation exposure; ultrasound is the appropriate modality. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Commence long-term low-dose trimethoprim prophylaxis at 2 mg/kg nocte and defer all renal tract imaging until age 2 years unless she develops a further breakthrough febrile urinary tract infection
options.rationale: Prophylaxis is reserved for recurrent UTI or significant reflux; deferring all imaging in an infant under 6 months misses tract anomalies that need follow-up. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Infants under 6 months with their first febrile UTI need a renal tract ultrasound during or shortly after the admission to look for obstruction, hydronephrosis or duplex anatomy. Routine MCUG and DMSA are not required after an uncomplicated treatment-responsive first UTI; both are added selectively for atypical features, abnormal ultrasound or recurrence. Long-term prophylaxis after a first uncomplicated UTI is not recommended. Children with confirmed high-grade reflux or significant scarring need nephrology input.
key_points: Ultrasound during admission is standard for infants under 6 months with febrile UTI
MCUG is reserved for abnormal ultrasound, atypical UTI or recurrence
DMSA is selective and not routine after a first uncomplicated UTI
CT and IV urogram are not first-line in paediatric UTI
Long-term prophylaxis is targeted, not universal
explanation.pearls: Atypical UTI features that escalate imaging include seriously unwell appearance, poor urine flow, non-E. coli organism, raised creatinine, sepsis or failure to respond within 48 hours of appropriate antibiotics.

subtopic_detail: Suspected meningococcal sepsis lumbar puncture timing
stem: You are the night paediatric registrar at a tertiary children's hospital. A 22-month-old non-Aboriginal boy has been transferred by retrieval after 8 hours of fever and a rapidly spreading purpuric rash on the trunk and limbs. He was alert in the field but is now drowsy, with rising oxygen requirements and prolonged capillary refill despite 40 mL/kg of crystalloid given pre-transfer. The retrieval team has commenced ceftriaxone empirically and asks what investigation pathway you want pursued on arrival.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 °C, HR 188, RR 48, SpO2 92% on 6 L Hudson, BP 78/40 mmHg, capillary refill 5 seconds, GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5)
data_table.Examination: Non-blanching purpuric rash on trunk and limbs with new lesions in the last hour, mottled peripheries, cool extremities, no neck stiffness, fontanelle closed, pupils equal and reactive
data_table.Investigations: Venous gas pH 7.18, lactate 6.4 mmol/L, glucose 4.2 mmol/L, FBC WCC 22 x10^9/L platelets 78 x10^9/L, INR 1.6, fibrinogen 1.0 g/L, CRP 220 mg/L, blood culture pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan for lumbar puncture?

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture in the emergency department before any further fluid resuscitation to obtain cerebrospinal fluid before any further antibiotic doses alter culture yield and direct narrow-spectrum therapy
options.rationale: Shock, coagulopathy and rapidly progressive purpura are contraindications to immediate LP in suspected meningococcal disease; blood and skin biopsy PCR retain diagnostic yield. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal disease)

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture immediately after intubation and a second bolus of crystalloid, sending samples for cell count culture and meningococcal polymerase chain reaction before any further antibiotic doses
options.rationale: Intubation does not remove the LP contraindications of coagulopathy and shock; antibiotics should not be delayed for any LP in this scenario. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Defer lumbar puncture for at least 24 hours and arrange a non-contrast computed tomography of the brain before any lumbar puncture is attempted, irrespective of haemodynamic and coagulation status at that point
options.rationale: Routine CT before LP does not exclude raised intracranial pressure and adds delay without changing the immediate threshold for LP. (Source: NICE NG143 Meningitis (bacterial) and meningococcal disease in under 16s)
source_refs: NICE NG143 Meningitis (bacterial) and meningococcal disease in under 16s

options.text: Defer lumbar puncture, continue empirical ceftriaxone, send blood for meningococcal polymerase chain reaction and serology and consider lumbar puncture later only once shock coagulopathy and rapidly evolving rash have resolved
options.rationale: Shock, coagulopathy (INR 1.6, fibrinogen 1.0), rapidly evolving purpura and GCS under 13 are all contraindications to LP; PCR on blood and skin biopsy plus blood culture obtains the microbiological diagnosis without delay. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal disease)

options.text: Defer lumbar puncture but discontinue ceftriaxone, switch to vancomycin and aciclovir empirically and recommence ceftriaxone only after blood polymerase chain reaction and culture results are available within 24 hours
options.rationale: Empirical ceftriaxone is the cornerstone of paediatric meningococcal sepsis therapy; switching to vancomycin plus aciclovir is not indicated in this clinical pattern. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in suspected meningococcal disease when there is shock, coagulopathy, rapidly evolving purpura, focal neurology, GCS under 13 or signs of raised intracranial pressure. Antibiotics should never be delayed for an LP in a deteriorating child. Blood culture, blood meningococcal PCR and skin lesion PCR have high yield in invasive meningococcal disease without requiring CSF. LP can be performed later once the child has stabilised and contraindications have resolved.
key_points: Shock, coagulopathy, GCS under 13 and rapidly evolving purpura contraindicate LP
Empirical ceftriaxone must not be delayed for any LP
Blood and skin PCR retain meningococcal diagnostic yield
Routine CT before LP does not reliably exclude raised ICP
LP can be deferred and performed once contraindications resolve
explanation.pearls: In paediatric meningococcal sepsis, fluid resuscitation past 40 mL/kg without haemodynamic response is the trigger for intubation, vasopressor support and admission to intensive care; dexamethasone is not given when the dominant picture is sepsis without proven bacterial meningitis.

sources.label: NICE NG143 Meningitis (bacterial) and meningococcal disease in under 16s

subtopic_detail: Suspected non-accidental injury workup in an infant
stem: You are the paediatric registrar admitting a 4-month-old non-Aboriginal infant brought in by his father for being unsettled. On examination the child has a spiral fracture of the left humerus, multiple bruises of varying ages on the trunk and back and a small frenular tear. The father's explanation is that the baby rolled off the lounge yesterday and was crying intermittently. The infant is otherwise developmentally appropriate and feeds normally. He is medically stable and analgesia has been given.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 148, RR 36, SpO2 99% room air, BP 86/50 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Spiral fracture left humerus on radiograph, three trunk bruises of varying ages, frenular tear, no external head injury or seizure activity, anterior fontanelle soft, pupils equal and reactive
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFT, coagulation profile within paediatric reference ranges, left humerus radiograph confirms spiral fracture, no other imaging performed yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate investigation pathway?

options.text: Admit for analgesia and arrange a full skeletal survey, fundoscopy by an ophthalmologist, non-contrast head computed tomography or magnetic resonance imaging and make a mandatory report to the child protection authority
options.rationale: Spiral long-bone fractures, frenular tear and unexplained multi-age bruising in a non-mobile infant mandate full child protection workup including skeletal survey, neuroimaging, fundoscopy and a statutory report. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)
source_refs: RACGP: Child Abuse Identification and Management

options.text: Splint the humeral fracture, organise outpatient orthopaedic follow-up within 1 week and discharge home with social work liaison through the general paediatric clinic the following morning
options.rationale: Discharge home in suspected non-accidental injury before completing the workup and statutory report places the child at ongoing risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Arrange a head computed tomography scan only, defer skeletal survey and ophthalmology until the family have given informed consent and discuss further investigation with the consultant paediatrician in the morning
options.rationale: Limiting imaging and deferring statutory reporting until family consent is obtained breaches mandatory reporting obligations and risks missed occult injury. (Source: RACGP: Child Abuse Identification and Management)
source_refs: RACGP: Child Abuse Identification and Management

options.text: Send extensive bone profile vitamin D and copper studies before any skeletal survey to first exclude metabolic bone disease as a cause of the spiral fracture, then reconsider child protection reporting in 48 hours
options.rationale: Metabolic bone disease is exceptionally rare and should not delay the standard non-accidental injury workup or mandatory reporting in this clinical pattern. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Arrange a full skeletal survey now but defer fundoscopy and head imaging until the infant develops neurological signs such as seizures, vomiting, lethargy or focal weakness on serial paediatric review
options.rationale: Occult abusive head trauma occurs without external signs; fundoscopy and neuroimaging are part of the routine workup, not symptom-triggered. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

explanation.summary: Suspected non-accidental injury in a pre-mobile infant requires a structured workup including skeletal survey, neuroimaging, fundoscopy by ophthalmology, baseline bloods and a statutory report. Frenular tears, posterior trunk bruising and spiral long-bone fractures in non-mobile infants are sentinel injuries with a high yield for occult injury. Mandatory reporting obligations apply to all medical practitioners in every Australian jurisdiction. Care planning and discharge decisions are made jointly with the child protection authority and not by the treating team alone.
key_points: Frenular tear, posterior bruising and spiral fracture in a non-mobile infant are sentinel for abuse
Workup is skeletal survey, neuroimaging, fundoscopy and baseline bloods
Mandatory reporting to the state child protection authority is required
Metabolic bone disease evaluation does not delay protective action
Discharge planning is joint with child protection
explanation.pearls: A skeletal survey repeat at 11-14 days improves yield for healing rib and metaphyseal fractures that are occult on the first set; classic metaphyseal lesions and posterior rib fractures are highly specific for non-accidental injury.

sources.label: RACGP: Child Abuse Identification and Management
